profile.summary: A Senior Product Designer owns end-to-end design for meaningful product areas, from discovery through shipped experience. They turn user and business needs into flows, prototypes, and polished UI, while shaping quality across a squad. They work closely with product managers, engineers, researchers, content, and design peers, and are often expected to influence roadmap, advocate for users, and raise craft standards beyond their own tickets.

known_challenges.text: Balancing fast delivery with enough research, iteration, and design quality.
Influencing product decisions without formal authority, especially when tradeoffs favor speed or scope.
Keeping a coherent user experience across teams, legacy patterns, and changing priorities.
Handling constant feedback from product, engineering, and design without losing clarity or confidence.
Stretching from strong execution into clearer strategic thinking and sharper product judgment.

recommended_question_themes.why: Senior designers are more effective when they help define the problem, not just polish solutions.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Tradeoffs between speed, quality, and user needs
recommended_question_themes.why: This role regularly has to negotiate what can ship now versus what protects the experience long term.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Influence with product and engineering partners
recommended_question_themes.why: A lot of success at this level depends on trust, alignment, and the ability to move decisions cross-functionally.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Craft growth and areas of stretch
recommended_question_themes.why: Senior designers are often judged on both execution quality and their ability to level up judgment and systems thinking.
Design work is highly visible and critique-heavy, so sustained pressure can quietly drain confidence and focus.

terminology.meaning: Step-by-step path a user takes through a task.
Low-detail layout used to explore structure and flow.
Clickable model used to test behavior and ideas.
Structured feedback on work to improve decisions.
Designing so more people can use the product.
Ongoing learning with users alongside delivery.
Shared components and rules for consistent product design.
Guiding outcome or experience direction for a product area.
Conscious choice between competing constraints or goals.
Working with different partners to align decisions.
Connecting user needs, business goals, and feasibility.
Accumulated experience issues left unresolved over time.
Sharing design intent and specs with engineering.
Checking built work matches intended experience.
Short delivery cycle used by product teams.
Common tool for interface design and collaboration.
Using AI tools to speed concept exploration.

listen_for: Whether they talk about problems and outcomes, not just screens and deliverables.
How they describe tradeoffs with product and engineering partners.
Whether they bring up user evidence or rely mainly on opinion.
Signs they are carrying invisible glue work like alignment, critique, or system cleanup.
Whether feedback sounds energizing, noisy, or personally draining.
avoid: Treating them like a UI decorator who joins after requirements are fixed.
Assuming more senior means they want less hands-on design work.
Pushing for speed without acknowledging research, accessibility, or design debt costs.
Judging impact only by shipped screens instead of influence on decisions and experience quality.
